Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd (in compulsory liquidation)

Facts.Text: Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd was incorporated on 8 April 2011.
Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd was in the business of chartering ships, tankers and vessels, as well as their management and operations.
Between 8 April 2011 and 26 March 2020, Wee See Boon was the local resident director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
Peter Christian Harren was one of the other two directors of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
Martin Harren was one of the other two directors of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
Mr Peter Christian Harren and Dr Martin Harren were both Germans and resided in Germany.
All three directors were signatories of the Plaintiff's DBS accounts.
The Plaintiff's former immediate holding company was Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.
The Plaintiff's former ultimate holding company was HPS International Holding GmbH.
The Defendant was also a director of HPSH.
The Defendant was a signatory of HPSH's bank account.
The Plaintiff had stopped generating revenue by the end of 2017.
Between 12 December 2018 and 27 December 2018, HPSH partially repaid a loan from the Plaintiff to HPSH. In total, HPSH made six payments amounting to US$1,461,000.

Between 12 December 2018 and 27 December 2018, payments amounting to the Principal Sum were made by the Plaintiff to HARPA Service & Support GmBH & Co. and HPS Shipping & Management GmbH & Co. KG in the respective sums of US$790,500 and US$682,000.
The service agreements signed by the Defendant were both dated 1 October 2018.
At the material time, Dr Harren was a director of HARPA and HPS.

In a letter from Luther LLP on 11 September 2020, on behalf of the three former directors, it was explained that the service providers 'reserved the right to settle the group-wide costs at a later stage'. As such, the costs of the services were settled in 2018. This was said to be a 'usual setup that a number of companies active in the business choose'.

In a letter from I.N.C Law LLC dated 15 June 2021 on behalf of the Defendant, it was explained that 'it is not uncommon to distribute project-related costs among the project sponsors at a later point in time'.
As of 31 December 2018, the Plaintiff had net liabilities of US$1,132,751.
Apart from HARPA and HPS, the Plaintiff owed OIG, a sum of S$1,805,568.10.

According to the Defendant, the Payments were in relation to services furnished by HARPA and HPS to the Plaintiff from March 2014 to August/September 2016 concerning the vessel Blue Giant in the Gulf of Mexico.
The winding up application was made on 19 April 2020 by OIG.

In a letter from Luther LLP on 1 December 2021, HARPA and HPS considered their claims against the Plaintiff to be well founded, but if the payments were seen to be giving them 'preferential treatment' over other creditors under Singapore law, they were prepared to repay the sums and 'justify their claims in the same way as the other creditors would do.'
On 23 December 2021, HARPA and HPS refunded the Principal Sum to the Plaintiff.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a former director is personally liable for authorizing or failing to prevent preferential payments made to related corporate entities while the company was insolvent.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the company and exercise reasonable care, skill, and diligence. When a company is insolvent, this duty extends to the company's creditors. A director may be held personally liable for breaches of these duties if they procure, authorize, or negligently fail to prevent transactions that constitute unfair preferences under insolvency law.
Party_Details.Application: In December 2018, while the plaintiff company was insolvent and carrying substantial debt, it made payments totaling US$1,472,500 to two related German entities. These payments settled past service debts but effectively preferred these related entities over other creditors (like OIG). The plaintiff argues that the defendant, Wee See Boon, as a director and bank signatory, breached his duties by failing to ascertain the permissibility of these payments or prevent his co-directors from authorizing them. The core dispute is whether the defendant's alleged inaction and lack of oversight regarding these unfair preference payments amounted to a breach of his fiduciary and statutory duties.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court characterized the December 2018 payments as unfair preferences under insolvency law. However, it held that the existence of an unfair preference does not automatically render a director personally liable for breach of fiduciary duty unless the director actually procured or knew of the payments. The court found no evidence that the defendant—a local resident director with limited administrative duties—knew or ought to have known about the specific transactions, which were authorized and executed by the German directors. Furthermore, the plaintiff failed to establish what specific oversight steps the defendant should have reasonably taken to prevent the payments. Without proof of actual knowledge or a failure to meet the objective standard of care for a resident director, the court found no breach of duty. Consequently, the claims for damages against the defendant were dismissed.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wee See Boon

Facts.Text: Between 8 April 2011 and 26 March 2020, Wee See Boon was the local resident director of Bit Baltic Investment & Trading Pte Ltd.
Wee See Boon was also a director of Harren & Partner Singapore Holding Pte Ltd.
Wee See Boon was a signatory of HPSH's bank account.
Wee See Boon was one of three signatories of the Plaintiff's DBS accounts.

The Defendant's written submissions stated that his primary responsibility as local resident director was to ensure the necessary paperwork with auditors, agencies and regulatory authorities in Singapore.
The Defendant contended that the German directors, Mr Peter Christian Harren and Dr Martin Harren, had full management and conduct of the Plaintiff's business operations and financial matters.
The service agreements signed by the Defendant were dated 1 October 2018.
The service agreements signed by the Defendant were dated 1 October 2018.

The Defendant said the Payments were in relation to services furnished by HARPA and HPS to the Plaintiff from March 2014 to August/September 2016 concerning the vessel Blue Giant in the Gulf of Mexico.

The Defendant contended that he was only subsequently informed by the Finance Manager, Ms Lim Woan Ching, that the Payments had been made, and that the Payments were approved by the German finance department via the authorised digitpass token.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a local resident director with limited administrative responsibilities breaches his fiduciary duties or duty of care by failing to prevent unfair preference payments executed by his foreign co-directors.
Party_Details.Rule: A director's liability for a company's unfair preference payments depends on their actual involvement and state of knowledge. A local resident director whose role is strictly administrative and who does not control the company's financial operations cannot be held personally liable for failing to prevent preferential payments unless it is proven that they knew, or reasonably ought to have known, of the transactions and failed to act.
Party_Details.Application: Wee See Boon served as the local resident director for the plaintiff company, while the German co-directors retained full control over operations and finances. During a period of insolvency, the company transferred US$1,472,500 to two related entities to settle service costs. Wee See Boon claimed he only learned of these transfers after they were executed via a banking token controlled by the German finance department. The legal conflict assesses whether Wee See Boon's failure to actively monitor the company's accounts and prevent these preferential payments constitutes a breach of his duty of care, skill, and diligence, thereby rendering him personally liable for the dissipated funds.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court dismissed the claims against the defendant, finding that he did not breach any fiduciary or statutory duties. The evidence demonstrated that the defendant's role was confined to local administrative compliance, while the German directors exercised exclusive control over the company's financial operations. The court ruled that the defendant did not have actual or constructive knowledge of the preferential payments prior to their execution. Distinguishing this case from precedents where directors actively procured or willfully ignored known preferential transfers, the court held that a local resident director is not automatically liable for the covert financial decisions of co-directors. Because the plaintiff failed to prove the defendant knew of the payments or articulate specific monitoring steps he should have reasonably undertaken, the claim for personal liability was dismissed.
